explicit_text: My grandfather, Kisaburo Konichi, was born around 1865 near Yokohama, Japan. His family was connected to the samurai class before the Meiji Restoration, with his father serving as a groom to a samurai. Kisaburo came to the United States in the 1890s, initially settling in San Francisco, where he experienced the earthquake and then moved to Sacramento. Later, he moved to Los Angeles and ran a go parlor from his living room, where he was known as a skilled go player and also made posters for community events. He died in the 1930s, and I never met him personally.

He died in the 1930s, and I never met him personally.

My grandfather's family was involved with the samurai class before the Meiji Restoration, and my grandmother’s family was well off, distributing Suntory whiskey. My grandparents eventually left Japan in the late 19th and early 20th centuries to settle in the United States. The cultural traditions and language from Japan were very important in our family, especially as they maintained Japanese language and customs after moving to America.

explicit_text: Yokohama was the birthplace of both my grandfather Kisaburo Konichi and my grandmother Chiyo Konishi. It was a city environment where my grandmother grew up, and she never learned to cook because they often bought food from vendors. This city background was part of their early life before they immigrated to the United States. Yokohama was also where my grandmother’s family was involved in the whiskey distribution business.

explicit_text: San Francisco was the first place my grandfather Kisaburo Konichi settled when he arrived in the United States in the 1890s. He was actually there during the famous earthquake, which frightened him so much that he left the city by ferry and then walked all the way to Sacramento. This experience marked the beginning of our family’s life in America.

It was an important early home for our family in America.

explicit_text: My grandmother, Chiyo Konishi, was born in Yokohama in 1890 to a family involved in distributing Suntory whiskey. After her mother died when she was young, a geisha who had been helping the family married her father, giving Chiyo a half-brother. She moved to the U.S. as a young woman and lived in Sacramento before moving to Little Tokyo in Los Angeles around 1913. She never learned English but adapted well, living with us throughout my childhood and taking care of the household while my parents worked hard.

explicit_text: Little Tokyo in Los Angeles was where my grandparents lived after moving from Sacramento. It was a vibrant Japanese community where my mother and aunt grew up and attended Amelia Street School. My grandfather’s go parlor was located there, and the neighborhood supported many cultural traditions, including Japanese language use at home and community events. It was also where my mother and aunt acted in Japanese films during their childhood.

explicit_text: My mother, Chie Konishi, was born in Sacramento in 1911 before our family moved to Los Angeles when she was about two years old. She grew up in Little Tokyo, attending Amelia Street School where speaking Japanese was forbidden, although her father insisted on Japanese at home. She and her younger sister Michiko acted in Japanese films as children, sometimes alongside famous actors like Sessue Hayakawa. My mother worked very hard throughout her life, and her upbringing was shaped by the strictness of her father and the dual cultural worlds she navigated.

explicit_text: Amelia Street School was the public grammar school my mother and aunt attended in Little Tokyo, Los Angeles. At school, they were not allowed to speak Japanese and had to use English, which created a cultural divide since their father insisted on Japanese at home. My mother recalled the penny lunches there, where students could buy milk and dessert for just a few cents. The school was an important part of their childhood experience growing up in the Japanese American community.

Although I don’t know much about the production companies, Hayakawa’s involvement connects our family to that unique cultural moment.
